title: Household hygiene and reducing spread
summary: Respiratory viruses mainly spread through shared air and close contact, with hands and contaminated surfaces also contributing. Layer a few realistic measures rather than trying to sterilise the whole house.
evidenceSnapshot: Staying home while acutely unwell, improving ventilation, covering coughs, handwashing and masking during close indoor contact are practical evidence-based measures.

interventions.name: Stay home and reduce close contact while acutely unwell
interventions.practical: Avoid work, school and vulnerable visitors while feverish or clearly infectious; follow current public-health advice.
interventions.mechanism: Reduces exposure dose and contact events.
interventions.cautions: Caregivers may still need close contact; add ventilation, hand hygiene and a well-fitting mask.

interventions.name: Ventilation and outdoor air
interventions.practical: Open suitable windows/doors, use exhaust fans and prefer outdoor or larger rooms when practical.
interventions.cautions: Balance with weather, smoke, security and child safety.

interventions.name: Well-fitting masks in close indoor contact
interventions.finding: Reduce emission and inhalation of respiratory particles.
interventions.practical: Use the best-fitting mask the person can tolerate, especially around vulnerable household members.
interventions.cautions: Replace wet or soiled masks; children require age-appropriate supervision.

interventions.name: Handwashing
interventions.finding: Reduces transfer from hands to eyes, nose, mouth and shared objects.
interventions.practical: Wash with soap and water for about 20 seconds, especially after nose-blowing and before food preparation.
interventions.mechanism: Soap lifts and disrupts microbes; rinsing removes them.
interventions.cautions: Alcohol hand rub is useful when hands are not visibly dirty.

interventions.name: High-touch surface cleaning
interventions.finding: Can reduce indirect transmission, especially in shared bathrooms and kitchens.
interventions.practical: Clean visibly dirty and frequently touched surfaces with ordinary detergent; disinfect when indicated.
interventions.mechanism: Removes or inactivates virus on surfaces.
interventions.cautions: Do not mix cleaning chemicals; ventilation matters more than obsessive whole-house disinfection.

practicalSteps: Give the sick person their own towel, drink bottle and toothbrush.
Place tissues and a bin near the resting area; wash hands after handling them.
Ventilate the main shared room and bathroom.
Avoid sharing food utensils before they are washed.
Clean phones, remote controls, taps, handles and bathroom surfaces routinely.
avoidOrCare: Do not spray disinfectant into the air or onto skin.
Do not mix bleach with ammonia, acids or other cleaners.
Avoid rigid isolation that is unsafe or emotionally harmful for a young child; use layered risk reduction instead.

myths.claim: Every surface must be disinfected constantly.
myths.reality: Shared air and close contact are major routes; targeted cleaning plus ventilation and hand hygiene is more proportionate.
Viruses cause colds; seasonal indoor crowding and environmental factors can affect transmission, but temperature alone is not the infection.

redFlags: A vulnerable household member develops symptoms — seek early medical advice
The caregiver becomes too unwell to provide safe care
Use of cleaning products causes breathing difficulty, eye injury or poisoning — move to fresh air and seek urgent advice
unsettled: The relative contribution of aerosols, droplets and surfaces varies by virus, setting and behaviour; layered controls remain more reliable than a single measure.
